content:
# Leviticus Chapter 7 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 60v–61r | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus pp. 91–92

---

**1** "And this is the instruction of the guilt offering — it is most holy.

**2** In the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered, they shall slaughter the guilt offering, and its blood shall be thrown against the altar on all sides.

**3** And all its fat he shall offer from it: the fat tail and the fat that covers the entrails,

**4** and the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the lobe of the liver — which he shall remove along with the kidneys.

**5** And the priest shall burn them on the altar as a fire offering to the LORD; it is a guilt offering.

**6** Every male among the priests may eat of it. It shall be eaten in a holy place — it is most holy.

**7** The guilt offering is like the sin offering; there is one instruction for them. The priest who makes atonement with it shall have it.

**8** And the priest who offers any man's burnt offering — that priest shall have for himself the skin of the burnt offering that he has offered.

**9** And every grain offering baked in an oven and every grain offering prepared on a pan or on a griddle shall belong to the priest who offers it.

**10** And every grain offering mixed with oil or dry shall belong to all the sons of Aaron, one as much as another.

---

**11** "And this is the instruction of the sacrifice of peace offerings that one may offer to the LORD:

**12** If he offers it as a thanksgiving offering, then he shall offer with the sacrifice of thanksgiving: unleavened loaves mixed with oil, unleavened wafers smeared with oil, and loaves of fine flour well mixed with oil.

**13** He shall bring his offering with loaves of leavened bread alongside the sacrifice of his thanksgiving peace offering.

**14** And from it he shall offer one from each offering as a contribution to the LORD — it shall belong to the priest who throws the blood of the peace offerings.

**15** And the flesh of the sacrifice of his thanksgiving peace offering shall be eaten on the day of his offering — he shall not leave any of it until morning.

**16** But if the sacrifice of his offering is a vow or a freewill offering, it shall be eaten on the day that he offers his sacrifice, and on the next day what remains of it shall also be eaten.

**17** But what remains of the flesh of the sacrifice on the third day shall be burned with fire.

**18** If any of the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offerings is eaten on the third day, it shall not be accepted — he who offers it shall not have it credited to him. It shall be a piggul, and the person who eats of it shall bear his iniquity.

**19** Flesh that touches any unclean thing shall not be eaten; it shall be burned with fire. As for the flesh, every clean person may eat the flesh.

**20** But the person who eats of the flesh of the sacrifice of peace offerings that belongs to the LORD while his uncleanness is upon him — that person shall be cut off from his people.

**21** And if anyone touches any unclean thing — whether the uncleanness of a human or an unclean animal or any unclean detestable thing — and eats of the flesh of the sacrifice of peace offerings that belongs to the LORD, that person shall be cut off from his people."

---

**22** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**23** "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying: You shall eat no fat of ox or sheep or goat.

**24** The fat of an animal that died of itself and the fat of one that is torn by beasts may be put to any other use, but you shall by no means eat it.

**25** For every person who eats fat from an animal of which a fire offering may be made to the LORD shall be cut off from his people.

**26** And you shall eat no blood whatever, whether of bird or of animal, in any of your dwelling places.

**27** Any person who eats any blood — that person shall be cut off from his people."

---

**28** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**29** "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying: Whoever offers the sacrifice of his peace offerings to the LORD shall bring his offering to the LORD from the sacrifice of his peace offerings.

**30** His own hands shall bring the fire offerings of the LORD — he shall bring the fat along with the breast, and the breast shall be waved as a wave offering before the LORD.

**31** And the priest shall burn the fat on the altar, but the breast shall belong to Aaron and his sons.

**32** And the right thigh you shall give as a contribution to the priest from the sacrifices of your peace offerings.

**33** Whoever among the sons of Aaron offers the blood of the peace offerings and the fat — the right thigh shall be his as a portion.

**34** For the breast of the wave offering and the thigh of the contribution I have taken from the sons of Israel, from the sacrifices of their peace offerings, and have given them to Aaron the priest and to his sons as a perpetual statute from the sons of Israel."

---

**35** This is the anointed portion of Aaron and the anointed portion of his sons from the fire offerings of the LORD, from the day they were presented to serve as priests to the LORD —

**36** which the LORD commanded to be given them from the sons of Israel, on the day that he anointed them. It is a perpetual statute throughout their generations.

**37** This is the instruction of the burnt offering, of the grain offering, of the sin offering, of the guilt offering, of the ordination offering, and of the sacrifice of peace offerings,

**38** which the LORD commanded Moses on Mount Sinai, on the day that he commanded the sons of Israel to bring their offerings to the LORD, in the wilderness of Sinai.

---

## Council Notes

### Structure and Overview

Leviticus 7 concludes the priestly sacrificial manual begun in Leviticus 1. The chapter divides into four sections: (1) the guilt offering supplement (vv. 1–10); (2) the peace offering instruction with its three sub-forms — thanksgiving (todah), vow (neder), and freewill offering (nedavah) — including purity regulations and the piggul law (vv. 11–21); (3) the universal fat and blood prohibitions for all Israel (vv. 22–27); and (4) the wave offering and contribution procedure with the perpetual priestly portions (vv. 28–36), followed by the summary colophon closing Leviticus 1–7 (vv. 37–38). MT and English versification are identical throughout.

### Key Term Resolutions

**"Instruction" (torah, vv. 1, 7, 11, 37).** All three agents rendered torah as "instruction" in the procedural-priestly context. SOPHIA maintains this convention consistently across all four occurrences, which is the established standard from prior chapters.

**"Guilt offering" (asham) vs. "sin offering" (chattat).** EMET and LOGOS consistently render chattat as "sin offering" and asham as "guilt offering." PNEUMA departs at v. 7, rendering chattat as "purification offering" and at v. 37 rendering chattat again as "purification offering." SOPHIA rules for "sin offering" throughout, maintaining the established project-wide convention (chattat = "sin offering," NOT "purification offering," as specified in the key term conventions). This is not a textual dispute but a terminological one; the project convention is clear.

**"Procedure" (PNEUMA) vs. "instruction" (EMET/LOGOS) for torah.** PNEUMA renders the opening formula in v. 1 and throughout as "procedure," while EMET and LOGOS use "instruction." SOPHIA rules for "instruction," which is the established project convention for torah in cultic contexts.

**"Thrown" / "dashed" / "splashed" for zaraq (v. 2).** EMET uses "dash"; LOGOS uses "thrown"; PNEUMA uses "splashed." The Hebrew zaraq implies a forceful throwing gesture (not a gentle sprinkling). SOPHIA adopts "thrown" (LOGOS), which best captures the vigor of zaraq while maintaining natural English. "Splash" (PNEUMA) is too casual and domestic; "dash" (EMET) is slightly archaic.

**"At the loins" vs. "which is by the flanks" vs. "near the loins" (v. 4).** EMET preserves "by the flanks" (ha-kesalim); LOGOS renders "at the loins"; PNEUMA uses "near the loins." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA with "at the loins" — a slight anatomical clarification that maps the Hebrew kesalim (flanks/loins) accurately without introducing the less familiar "flanks."

**"May eat of it" vs. "shall eat it" (v. 6).** LOGOS and PNEUMA use "may eat" (permissive), rendering the imperfect more naturally. EMET uses "shall eat it." SOPHIA adopts "may eat of it" (LOGOS) — the permission nuance is appropriate for a regulation granting priestly access, not issuing a command to consume.

**"The guilt offering is like the sin offering" vs. "Like the sin offering, so is the guilt offering" (v. 7).** EMET arranges the comparison as "Like the sin offering, so is the guilt offering"; LOGOS and PNEUMA lead with "The guilt offering is like the sin offering." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA for more natural English word order, keeping the subject (guilt offering) as topic given this chapter's focus.

**"Abomination" / "piggul" / "offensive" (v. 18).** EMET preserves piggul transliterated with a gloss "rejected thing / foul thing"; LOGOS renders "abomination"; PNEUMA renders "offensive." SOPHIA chooses to retain "piggul" as a transliterated technical term — it appears only three times in the Hebrew Bible (Lev 7:18; 19:7; Ezek 4:14), has no single adequate English equivalent, and is defined by its immediate context. This is consistent with the project's treatment of other technical terms (massebah, El Shaddai). Rendering it "abomination" (LOGOS) imports connotations of moral repugnance that obscure its specific technical meaning (sacrificial invalidity through improper priestly intention); "offensive" (PNEUMA) is too mild. The text's own context explains it: the flesh is "not accepted," "not credited," and the eater "bears iniquity." Piggul stands in the translation as a defined technical term.

**"Shall bear his iniquity" vs. "will bear his own guilt" (v. 18).** EMET and LOGOS render avonah yissa as "shall bear his/her iniquity." PNEUMA renders "will bear his own guilt." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "bear his iniquity" is the standard Levitical idiom for retained culpability and should be rendered consistently with its other occurrences. "Guilt" for avon slightly collapses the Hebrew distinction between avon (iniquity/culpability) and asham (guilt/guilt offering).

**"Person" vs. "life" / "soul" (nefesh, vv. 18, 20, 21, 25, 27).** EMET uses "life" (a direct rendering of nefesh); LOGOS and PNEUMA use "person." SOPHIA adopts "person" for readability in legal contexts. The Hebrew nefesh encompasses the whole living person; "person" captures this without the mystical connotations of "soul" or the biological narrowness of "life" in modern English.

**"People of Israel" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) vs. "sons of Israel" (EMET) for bney yisrael (vv. 23, 29, 38).** EMET preserves the literal "sons of Israel," which is also the project convention from Genesis onward. LOGOS and PNEUMA use "people of Israel" and "Israelites" for readability. SOPHIA rules for "sons of Israel" in the speech-opening formula (vv. 23, 29) and "sons of Israel" also in vv. 34, 36, maintaining the literal convention established in Genesis. At v. 38, "sons of Israel" is maintained consistently.

**"Cattle, sheep, or goat" vs. "ox or sheep or goat" (v. 23).** PNEUMA renders shor as "cattle"; EMET and LOGOS render it "ox." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: shor is the Hebrew for a bovine, most naturally "ox" in sacrificial contexts (the specific adult male or bovine animal offered). "Cattle" is too generic.

**"Died naturally" / "died of itself" / "died naturally or been killed" for nevela (v. 24).** EMET renders "animal that died naturally"; LOGOS renders "animal that dies of itself"; PNEUMA renders "died naturally." SOPHIA adopts "animal that died of itself" (LOGOS) — the precise legal concept is death apart from slaughter, which "dies of itself" conveys precisely.

**"Torn by beasts" / "torn by wild animals" for terefah (v. 24).** EMET renders "what was torn"; LOGOS renders "one that is torn by beasts"; PNEUMA renders "killed by wild animals." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "torn by beasts" is the standard rendering and captures the Hebrew terefah (lit. "torn"). "Killed" (PNEUMA) obscures the specific tearing/mauling character of the death.

**"Waved as a wave offering" vs. "elevated as a wave offering" for le-hanif tenufah (v. 30).** EMET uses "to wave as a wave offering"; LOGOS uses "waved as a wave offering"; PNEUMA uses "elevated as a wave offering." SOPHIA adopts "waved as a wave offering," matching the established term tenufah = "wave offering" and maintaining the consistent verbal connection. The MT root nuf denotes a waving/swinging motion.

**"Perpetual due" (EMET) vs. "perpetual statute" (LOGOS) vs. "permanent share" / "permanent obligation" (PNEUMA) for chuqat olam (vv. 34, 36).** EMET renders "perpetual due"; LOGOS renders "perpetual statute"; PNEUMA renders "permanent share" (v. 34) and "permanent obligation" (v. 36). SOPHIA adopts "perpetual statute" (LOGOS) consistently — it renders chuqat (statute, ordinance) accurately and maintains formal register. "Due" (EMET) adds an interpretation; "obligation" and "share" (PNEUMA) split what is one Hebrew phrase.

**"Anointed portion" for mishchat (v. 35).** All three agents agree on this rendering. SOPHIA maintains it.

**"Ordination offering" for milu'im (v. 37).** All three agents agree. SOPHIA maintains it.

**"Sacrifice of peace offerings" vs. "peace offering" for zevach ha-shelamim (v. 37 colophon).** EMET and LOGOS render the full "sacrifice of peace offerings." PNEUMA shortens to "peace offering." SOPHIA renders the full "sacrifice of peace offerings" — the colophon is a formal summary and should preserve the full technical designation.

### Theological Observations

**The todah as the crown of sacrifice.** The chapter's centerpiece — the thanksgiving peace offering with its extraordinary bread requirement and its one-day eating rule — occupies a place of structural priority within the peace offering material. Rabbinic tradition across multiple sources holds that in the age to come, all offerings will cease except the todah, because gratitude will remain even when sin is abolished. The LXX's rendering of todah as ainesis ("praise") — adopted by the New Testament in Hebrews 13:15 ("sacrifice of praise") — shows how this one Levitical sub-category became a template for understanding eschatological worship. The todah's combination of leavened and unleavened bread is unique in the entire Levitical system: the unleavened bread connects to altar purity, the leavened bread to ordinary domestic life, making the thanksgiving offering the one sacrifice that spans both the sacred and the everyday.

**Piggul and the constitutive power of priestly intention.** The piggul law (v. 18) reveals something unusual within the priestly system: the officiant's mental intention during the ritual procedure is constitutive of the offering's validity. A wrong intention — planning to eat the flesh beyond its lawful time — retroactively invalidates an otherwise perfectly performed sacrifice. This is the priestly legal system's clearest statement that ritual action and interior disposition are not separable. Mishnah Zevachim 2:2–4 extends this into an elaborate analysis, but the foundational principle is here in Leviticus 7.

**Fat and blood as divine reservations.** The fat and blood prohibitions (vv. 22–27) are extended explicitly to all Israel in all their dwelling places, not merely to sacrificial contexts. The chelev (visceral fat reserved for God's altar) and the blood (which "is the life," as Lev 17:11 will state) are together the two categories that God alone claims from every animal. Every act of meat preparation by any Israelite anywhere was a theological act: it recalled that the richest substance and the animating substance of every creature belong to God.

**The colophon's Sinai anchor.** The closing colophon (vv. 37–38) grounds the entire sacrificial system — all six offering types catalogued across Leviticus 1–7 — at Sinai, in the wilderness. The colophon makes an implicit claim against any reading that might understand the priestly offering code as a later development, a royal-era innovation, or an adaptation of Canaanite practice. The wilderness setting is theologically significant: these laws were given to a landless people in a desert, for a portable sanctuary, before Israel had any temple or any territory. The offering system is constitutively a wilderness document, designed for dependence.

---

*SOPHIA — synthesized from EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), and PNEUMA (dynamic)*
